title: I Ask You to Compare Them and See Clearly!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The standalone OCR line `ஸ்ர. ப... le EET` does not appear in the printed source and is removed from the derived Tamil reading copy. Source comparison also confirms repairs to `அன்றி`, `படிப்பவர்கள்`, `வெளியிடவில்லை`, `கல்விக் கடன்`, `நிறைவேற்றப்பட்டுள்ளன`, `நாடாளுமன்றத் தேர்தல்`, `பெங்களூரில்`, and `என்பதையும்`. Figures, institutional names and the quoted Vajpayee statement follow the source. The printed salutation is restored; canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam General Council met at Anna Arivalayam from 10 a.m. until 8 p.m. on 15 December 2013. Apart from the condolence resolution, twenty-four resolutions were adopted with the members' approval. Fifteen people spoke, including the parliamentary party leader, Members of Parliament, district secretaries and General Council members. After the Treasurer and General Secretary spoke, I delivered the concluding address. This is the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam.
The AIADMK Executive Committee and General Council both met on 19 December 2013.
The Executive Committee began at 3 p.m. and ended in five minutes. The General Council then began and concluded at 6.25 p.m. Sixteen resolutions were read and adopted in the interval.

Among the DMK's twenty-four resolutions were one mourning Nelson Mandela; one paying heroic tribute to Rajamurugan, who died during the Yercaud by-election; one appreciating this humble person for writing the sixth volume of *Nenjukku Needhi*; one thanking Yercaud's voters; one authorising the President and General Secretary to form an electoral alliance; and one concerning party organisational elections. Eight appealed to the Union government on matters including Eelam Tamils and Indian fishermen. Ten made requests to, or censured, the AIADMK government of Tamil Nadu. That makes twenty-four.

No one can point to a single word in any resolution that violates civility or political culture. Readers will recognise that all twenty-four were drafted with the proper character of political resolutions.
But what were the AIADMK's resolutions like? Of its sixteen:

* Four censured the Union government. * One concerned organisational elections. * Ten wished “Amma” a happy birthday, authorised her to decide alliances, and praised her for various reasons. * The one remaining resolution was for me!

The longest of the sixteen resolutions was the one that performed an “archanai” in my honour. Apart from the ruling party's official newspaper, no publication printed it in full because it was not civil enough to publish.

Its title was: “An affectionate appeal to Tamil Nadu's voters to reject completely in the coming parliamentary general election the evil force Karunanidhi, who, despite participating in the Union government for many years, did not perform a single good deed and betrayed Tamils in a manner that caused pain in the hearts of Tamils throughout the world.”

The long resolution harshly called me a “traitor to the race,” a “great sinner,” a “self-seeker,” an “evil force,” and a “traitor to the Tamil people.” My crime, it seems, was that the DMK did not perform one good deed while participating in the Union government. Here is the list!

It was while the DMK participated in the National Front government led by guardian of social justice V.P. Singh that the Mandal recommendation was accepted and twenty-seven per cent reservation introduced in Union government employment; the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal was constituted; and the international and domestic terminals at Chennai airport were named respectively after Arignar Anna and Perunthalaivar Kamarajar.

During the United Front government, when our departed younger brother Murasoli Maran served as Union Industry Minister in the Gujral cabinet, he designated nine outstanding public-sector enterprises as the “Navaratnas” and stimulated India's economic development.

It was under the National Democratic Alliance government led by Vajpayee that the Sethusamudram project took shape and permission was obtained to build a memorial for Perunthalaivar Kamarajar on the Kanyakumari seafront.

It was while the DMK held office in the United Progressive Alliance government led by Sonia Gandhi that Tamil obtained Classical Language status and the Central Institute of Classical Tamil was established in Chennai.
Through the Shipping and Highways Ministry:

* Of 4,676 kilometres of national highways, 3,276 kilometres were upgraded to four lanes at a cost of 56,664.21 crore rupees. * Major flyovers, port-expansion works, container terminals and inland-water transport facilities were established. * The Union government's National Automotive Testing and R&D Infrastructure Project centre was established at Oragadam near Chennai with an investment of 470 crore rupees. * Salem Steel Plant was upgraded to international standards at a cost of 1,553 crore rupees, and a new cold-rolling mill was created. * The National Institute of Siddha was established at Tambaram. * A new railway division was established at Salem. * Salem Government Mohan Kumaramangalam Medical College Hospital was upgraded to a super-speciality hospital at a cost of 120 crore rupees. * Work began on the Chennai Port-Maduravoyal elevated road at a cost of 1,650 crore rupees. * Sethusamudram project work began at a cost of 2,427 crore rupees. * CENVAT was abolished for the great benefit of the weaving community. * Another seawater desalination project was created at Nemmeli at a cost of 908 crore rupees. * The POTA law was repealed. * The National e-Governance Programme was introduced to establish complete computerised administration in every village. * A facility to call anywhere in India for fifty paise was introduced. * Twelve million new telephone connections were provided each month. * Mobile-phone charges were reduced. * Approval was obtained to convert every metre-gauge railway line in Tamil Nadu to broad gauge. * Approval was obtained for ninety railway overbridges costing 1,828 crore rupees. * The Chennai Metro Rail project was established. * The Hogenakkal Combined Drinking Water Project was established. * Twenty-seven per cent reservation was introduced in Union government educational institutions. * Tiruchi, Coimbatore and Madurai airports were expanded. * The Indian Maritime University of international standard was established near Chennai; a Central University at Tiruvarur; a world-class Central University at Coimbatore; and the Indian Institute of Management at Tiruchi. * Asia's first National Institute for Empowerment of Persons with Multiple Disabilities was established near Chennai. * A National Security Guard hub was established in Chennai. * The Union government waived 72,000 crore rupees in principal and interest owed by farmers to cooperative institutions and banks across India. * Educational loans worth several hundred crore rupees have been extended to students across India through nationalised banks.

Tamil Nadu's thirteen ministers in the Union government implemented numerous programmes for India's economic development and Tamil Nadu's economic progress. Of projects with a total value of 3.70 lakh crore rupees, Tamil Nadu received an eleven-per-cent share, a level never achieved in the past. Sixty-nine Union government projects were implemented in Tamil Nadu with more funding than the state had ever previously received.
I have given the list of good deeds performed while the DMK participated in the Union government. What, at the same time, is the AIADMK's record?

After the 1998 parliamentary election, when a BJP-led coalition government was formed, AIADMK leader Jayalalithaa, whose party had won as part of that alliance, deliberately delayed and stubbornly withheld even the letter of support that had to be sent to the President.
Her principal demand was that the DMK government in Tamil Nadu be dismissed. She also demanded the withdrawal of cases against her.

While part of the BJP coalition government, Jayalalithaa personally wrote and submitted recommendations demanding that the suspensions of former Chief Secretary Hari Bhaskar, IAS, and C. Ramachandran, IAS, be revoked; that Thoothukudi Port Trust Chairman S. Machendranathan be transferred to Paradip Port; and that, on the basis of a list already provided, P.K. Bansal, Registrar of Companies in Bengaluru, be appointed Joint Director in Mumbai, among several others.

Angered because everything she desired for her personal benefit was not fulfilled, Jayalalithaa met the President, withdrew support from the BJP government and became the foremost cause of the fall of the Union government led by Vajpayee.
Do you know the certificate Prime Minister Vajpayee gave her, as published in *Dinamani* on 22 August 1999?

> The period of alliance with the AIADMK was the most painful period of my political life. Even on the day before Independence Day, she threatened to bring down the government. But we did not yield to her “blackmail” tactics.
Because I have written this, no one should launch an imaginary campaign claiming I did so to form an alliance with the BJP.

I have explained these matters only so that people may compare how the DMK acted while sharing responsibility in the Union government with how the AIADMK acted when it did so. From this explanation, is it now clear who deserves the AIADMK resolution's labels of “evil force,” “self-seeker” and “traitor to the Tamil people” for failing to perform any good deed while in Union office?
Those who seek an impartial view should read the DMK and AIADMK General Council resolutions in full, compare them once, and see clearly for themselves.
With affection,
M.K.
